title: PDF Standards Ecosystem Reference
purpose: Complete map of PDF-related ISO standards, their relationships, version history, requirements, and how they relate to accessibility engineering.

coreSpecification.title: PDF Core Specification (ISO 32000)
coreSpecification.overview: The base PDF specification defines the file format, object model, content streams, fonts, graphics, and all foundational features. All other PDF standards (PDF/A, PDF/UA, PDF/X) are subsets or profiles built on top of this.

pdf14.label: PDF 1.4
pdf14.significance: Foundation for PDF/A-1. First version with robust tagged PDF support.

pdf15.label: PDF 1.5
pdf15.significance: Cross-reference streams enable smaller tagged PDFs. Object streams compress the many small objects that tagged PDF generates.

pdf16.label: PDF 1.6
pdf16.significance: Native OpenType support improves font handling for accessibility.

pdf17.label: PDF 1.7
pdf17.significance: First fully ISO-standardized PDF. Foundation for PDF/UA-1 and PDF/A-2/3. This is the version most of our pipeline targets.
pdf17.ourUsage: Our pipeline produces PDF 1.7 output. PdfTagWriterCli sets /Version to 1.7. veraPDF validates against PDF/UA-1 which is based on this.

pdf20.label: PDF 2.0
keyFeatures: No more Standard 14 fonts (all fonts must be embedded)
Improved tagged PDF with new structure elements (Aside, DocumentFragment, Sub, etc.)
Associated files for embedding related content
pdf20.significance: Major evolution. PDF/UA-2 and PDF/A-4 are built on this. The 'no Standard 14 fonts' rule aligns with what our font-embedder already enforces.
breakingChanges: Standard 14 fonts officially removed (we already substitute with Noto)
pdf20.ourReadiness: Our pipeline enforces font embedding (PDF 2.0 compatible) but structure elements are currently limited to the PDF 1.7 set. Expanding to PDF 2.0 structure elements is a future enhancement.

accessibilityStandards.title: PDF/UA — Universal Accessibility (ISO 14289)
accessibilityStandards.overview: PDF/UA defines how tagged PDF should be used to create accessible documents. It requires proper structure, reading order, font embedding, and alternative text.

pdfUA1.label: PDF/UA-1
pdfUA1.purpose: Universal Accessibility requirements for PDF 1.7 documents.

keyRequirements.requirement: Document metadata: /Lang required, XMP dc:title required, PDF/UA identification in XMP
All content must be tagged or marked as Artifact
keyRequirements.ourStatus: Implemented — raster mode wraps pages as Artifact + overlay; native mode tags all operators

keyRequirements.requirement: Structure types must be semantically appropriate (P for paragraphs, H1-H6 for headings, etc.)
keyRequirements.ourStatus: Implemented — semantic engine assigns correct roles

keyRequirements.requirement: Logical reading order must be deterministic and match visual order
keyRequirements.ourStatus: Implemented — reading-order module with line-group epsilon

keyRequirements.requirement: Table structure: TH cells must have Scope attribute
keyRequirements.ourStatus: Implemented — inferTableScope guarantees Scope on every TH

keyRequirements.requirement: Complex tables should use Headers attribute on TD cells
Form fields must be accessible (names, tooltips)
keyRequirements.ourStatus: Partially implemented — /DA font embedding works, field naming is basic

keyRequirements.requirement: All fonts must be embedded with valid encoding
keyRequirements.ourStatus: Fully implemented — font-embedder + CIDSet fix + ToUnicode reconstruction

keyRequirements.requirement: CIDSet must enumerate all CIDs in the font program
keyRequirements.ourStatus: Implemented — CIDSet removed from subsetted Type0 fonts via saveIncremental

pdfUA2.label: PDF/UA-2
pdfUA2.purpose: Universal Accessibility requirements for PDF 2.0 documents. Completely rewritten, NOT backward compatible with PDF/UA-1.
keyDifferences: New structure elements: Aside, FENote, DocumentFragment, Sub, Em, Strong
Comprehensive requirements for structure element attributes (not just Scope on TH)
Comprehensive requirements for annotations (links, form fields, etc.)
Namespace-based structure types (can define custom namespaces)
Ruby and Warichu support for CJK annotations
breakingChanges: Standard 14 fonts no longer exist in PDF 2.0
Structure element naming follows PDF 2.0 conventions
pdfUA2.coexistence: PDF/UA-1 and PDF/UA-2 coexist. PDF/UA-1 is for PDF 1.7 documents, PDF/UA-2 is for PDF 2.0 documents. Validators check conformance to one or the other, not both.
pdfUA2.ourReadiness: Our pipeline currently targets PDF/UA-1. Migrating to PDF/UA-2 requires: (1) output PDF 2.0, (2) expand structure element types, (3) update XMP identification, (4) update veraPDF flavour to ua2.
migrationPath: 1. Update pdf-writer to output PDF 2.0 header (%PDF-2.0)
2. Add new structure element types (Aside, FENote, Em, Strong) to tag-builder
3. Update XMP metadata to declare PDF/UA-2 conformance
5. Set veraPDF flavour to ua2 (when veraPDF supports it)

archivalStandards.title: PDF/A — Archival (ISO 19005)
archivalStandards.overview: PDF/A defines subsets of PDF suitable for long-term preservation. Key restrictions: all fonts embedded, no encryption, no external dependencies, XMP metadata required.

pdfA1.label: PDF/A-1

conformanceLevels.a: Full tagged structure required (accessibility-level). Includes logical structure and Unicode mapping.
conformanceLevels.b: Visual reproduction only. No structure or Unicode required. 'Basic' level.

keyRestrictions: All fonts must be embedded (including CIDSet for CID fonts)
No transparency (this is the big limitation)
pdfA1.ourRelevance: PDF/A-1a requires CIDSet (which our pipeline removes for PDF/UA). These standards can conflict — a document cannot easily conform to both PDF/A-1a and PDF/UA-1 on CIDSet handling.

conformanceLevels.a: Full structure + Unicode (like PDF/A-1a but allows transparency)
conformanceLevels.u: Unicode text guaranteed (new level — text can be reliably extracted)

pdfA3.label: PDF/A-3
pdfA3.keyDifference: Identical to PDF/A-2 but allows ANY file type as attachment (not just PDF/A). Used for ZUGFeRD invoices (PDF with embedded XML).

pdfA4.label: PDF/A-4
keyChanges: Eliminates the confusing a/b/u conformance levels — just PDF/A-4 (optionally with tags)
PDF/A-4f allows embedded files of any format
PDF/A-4e allows 3D content, rich media, embedded files (engineering focus)

archivalStandards.ourArchivalSupport: Our pipeline does not currently target PDF/A compliance. Adding PDF/A support would require: ensuring no JavaScript/encryption, validating all fonts embedded (already done), adding PDF/A XMP identification, and running veraPDF with --flavour a2b or similar.

printStandards.title: PDF/X — Print Production (ISO 15930)
printStandards.overview: PDF/X ensures reliable print reproduction. Requires color management, embedded fonts, and no transparency (in older versions).

pdfX1a.label: PDF/X-1a
pdfX1a.keyRequirement: CMYK/spot colors only, no transparency, all fonts embedded

pdfX3.label: PDF/X-3
pdfX3.keyRequirement: Like X-1a but allows device-independent color (ICC profiles)

pdfX4.label: PDF/X-4
pdfX4.keyRequirement: Allows transparency and layers. OpenType fonts supported.

pdfX6.label: PDF/X-6
pdfX6.keyRequirement: PDF 2.0 based. Latest print production standard.

printStandards.ourRelevance: Not directly relevant to our accessibility pipeline, but understanding PDF/X helps when processing documents originally created for print that need accessibility tagging.

pdfE.label: PDF/E
pdfE.purpose: Engineering documents. Supports 3D content, geospatial data.

pdfVT.label: PDF/VT
pdfVT.purpose: Variable data and transactional printing. Extends PDF/X for high-volume personalized output.

pdfR.label: PDF/raster
pdfR.purpose: Subset for raster-only content. Ironically, our raster mode output is closest to this — pages as images.

standardRelationships.title: How Standards Relate
standardRelationships.hierarchy: ISO 32000 (PDF core) → PDF/UA (accessibility) + PDF/A (archival) + PDF/X (print). Each is a SUBSET — it restricts what PDF features can be used.
conflicts: PDF/A-1a requires CIDSet on subsetted CID fonts; PDF/UA-1 says if CIDSet exists it must be complete. Our fix (remove CIDSet) satisfies PDF/UA but may violate PDF/A-1a.
PDF/A prohibits encryption; some organizations need both archival and encrypted PDFs.
PDF/X requires specific color profiles; PDF/UA doesn't care about color management but requires contrast for accessibility.

dualConformance.pdfUA1_pdfA2a: Possible but requires careful CIDSet handling, complete structure tree, and all PDF/A-2a restrictions.
dualConformance.pdfUA2_pdfA4: Both based on PDF 2.0 — dual conformance is more natural. The PDF Association published guidance on this.
dualConformance.recommendation: For our pipeline: target PDF/UA-1 first (current), add PDF/A-2u as an optional output flavour (font embedding already satisfied, need XMP and restriction checks).

timeline.event: PDF 1.7 (ISO 32000-1) — PDF becomes an open ISO standard
PDF 2.0 (ISO 32000-2) — major revision, no more Standard 14 fonts
